title: Understanding Concepts
excerpt:
The Secret to Better Coding

When you're learning to code, the "concept" is the most important thing to grasp. You can think of it as the core logic or the formula...
markdown:
The Secret to Better Coding

When you're learning to code, the "concept" is the most important thing to grasp. You can think of it as the core logic or the formula behind a feature.

To break it down, here is a simple example: **a + b = x**

To get the result **x**, you have to understand that you need **a** and **b** first. That's the concept—knowing exactly what you need to achieve your goal before you even touch the keyboard.

### Why does this matter?

Here are three reasons why focusing on the concept is a game-changer:

1. **You know your end goal:** You aren't just typing random code; you have a clear vision of what you are building.

2. **You know your dependencies:** Just like in the math example, you identify exactly what inputs or data you need to reach the result.

3. **You have intentionality:** This is the most important part—you actually understand *what* you are doing and *why* you are doing it, rather than just copying and pasting from tutorials.

### The Bottom Line

By mastering the concept first, your coding and problem-solving skills will become so much stronger. It's easy to get lost in syntax, but if you remember to **"Understand the Concept,"** everything else will fall into place naturally.

title: What is an API?
excerpt:
Hi everyone! Sorry for the radio silence, lately it's been a busy few weeks. Let’s dive straight back into it with our next post.

**Let’s get straight to the point:** An **API (Application Program...
markdown:
Hi everyone! Sorry for the radio silence, lately it's been a busy few weeks. Let’s dive straight back into it with our next post.

**Let’s get straight to the point:** An **API (Application Programming Interface)** is simply a way for different apps to talk to each other. No hidden tricks, no fluff—just a direct line of communication.

### The Real-World Example
I bet you’ve seen pages that allow you to "Login with Google" or "Login with Facebook." How do they do it? You guessed it: **APIs.**

Imagine we have **Application A** and **Application B**:
1. **A** wants to get some info from **B**, but they are separate companies.
2. **B** is willing to share that data, provided certain terms and conditions are met.
3. **B** prepares an API and writes documentation on how to use it.
4. **A** follows those instructions to make the connection. 


### What does an API actually look like?
In many cases, an API looks like a URL (often called an *endpoint*). For example:
`https://api.example.com/v1/users`

But it’s rarely that easy to access. If an API were completely open, anyone could use (or abuse) the data. To keep things safe, developers use **Authentication**. They will provide you with a unique **Token** or "Key" to prove you have permission to be there. 

**Pro-tip:** Always refer to the documentation to get a clear picture of the security requirements.

### Conclusion
An API is a gateway that provides information through a structured, secure connection. While we often use them to connect different companies, they are also used within the same application to help different parts of the system stay organized.

**Remember:** An API is just another way of communicating. Hope this clears things up!

***

*Please leave a comment if you have any questions or if there's a specific tech topic you'd like me to cover next!*

title: How Important Reading is
excerpt:
# The Underrated Superpower: The Art of Reading

In the world of programming, what does "reading" actually mean? It means more than just glancing at words. Reading error messages and documentation is vital. It is the fuel that allows...
markdown:
# The Underrated Superpower: The Art of Reading

In the world of programming, what does "reading" actually mean? It means more than just glancing at words. Reading error messages and documentation is vital. It is the fuel that allows us to move forward quickly when we hit a wall. Reading isn’t just a task; it’s a time-saving strategy that allows you to react and solve problems with speed.

### Read to the Very End

One of the most important habits you can build is reading to the end of a document or a StackOverflow answer. Why? Because the solution is often in the context. Understanding the end result *before* you start typing helps you avoid wrong turns.

**If you don’t understand what you’re reading:**

* **Read it in "slow-mo":** Break down the sentences word by word.
* **Step away:** If you’re still stuck, take a break. Have a snack, get some fresh air, and come back to it.
* **Try again:** Read it slowly once more. Trust me, it works every time.

### A Habit with Life-Changing Side Effects

Developing a habit of technical reading carries over into your daily life. You’ll find yourself more attentive when buying products, signing up for services, or reviewing contracts.

However, remember this simple logic:

* **Reading without understanding = 0**
* **Reading + Understanding = 1**

When you truly understand, you are at the "true gate," and you can finally move forward with confidence.

### Conclusion

Read until the end. Read to understand. Take breaks when needed. This practice will lift you up, save you hours of debugging, and ultimately make you a much better team player.

So, cheer up and start reading before you start implementing!

title: Git
excerpt: Git is an awesome tool that is used by programmers all around the world. It was an amazing creation by the founder of Linux. It is a time machine that can...
markdown:
Git is an awesome tool that is used by programmers all around the world. It was an amazing creation by the founder of Linux. It is a time machine that can bring back any of your code from the past with just a few commands.

How do programmers work together in the real world? Don’t say they transfer their code via thumb drives?? definitely not, right? Git is the place where they host their code and collaborate with their colleagues.

You can install Git on your own server, or you can use services like www.github.com, www.bitbucket.org, and others. By now, I hope you guys understand what Git is. Now, let’s go to the next step.

### Repository (Repo)
'Repository,' or 'repo' for short, is equivalent to a folder—a folder that stores your source code. You can have multiple repos. Each repo gets its own URL, which you can share with your colleagues to start collaborating. That’s all about repos for this post. Explore it yourself and ask me if you have any questions!

### GitHub and Bitbucket
These two are cloud services that handle Git for you. As mentioned in my previous post, go ahead and search or read up about GitHub and Bitbucket. In this post, I will just give you guys a few hints.

**Bitbucket:** It's great for private repos and allows up to 5 people to collaborate for free. For more details, you guys can check out the pricing section on their site. What I really want to mention is the UX, in my personal opinion, Bitbucket's UX is so neat and really easy to use. You guys should check it out.


**GitHub:** They give free repos too, but with some limitations (check it out on their site). GitHub is really good for hosting static websites (e.g., https://skolacode.github.io/). GitHub's main intention is to help you share your knowledge with others, so they prefer you to make your repos public. It is used by developers around the globe to share their code.

Soon, I will publish more posts about Git features (conflicts, tags, rollbacks, etc.). Stay tuned guys!
